abstract:
This article presents an exegetical and theological analysis of the final pericope of the Book of Sirach (Sir 51:13-30), focusing on the Greek version as the canonical text. The passage is structured into two distinct sections: an autobiographical confession (vv. 13-22) and a didactic exhortation (vv. 23-30). In the first part, the sage recounts his lifelong commitment to seeking wisdom, emphasizing that this pursuit must begin in youth and continue until death. He identifies several essential means for finding wisdom, including persistent prayer, liturgical devotion, adherence to the Law, moral purification, and intellectual discipline.

In the second part, the author assumes the role of a master, inviting the uneducated to join his "house of instruction" (school) to partake in the wisdom he has acquired. The analysis highlights Sirach’s profound optimism regarding the accessibility of wisdom—positioning his view as a counterpoint to the more skeptical outlooks found in the Books of Job and Ecclesiastes. Ultimately, the article argues that Sir 51:13-30 serves as a theological synthesis of the entire book. By mirroring themes introduced in the opening chapter (Sir 1:1-10), this concluding pericope forms a literary inclusion that frames the work, reinforcing the inseparable link between the acquisition of wisdom, the fear of the Lord, and the observance of the Mosaic Law.
